questions.question: Define Point Light Source and list its essential properties.
keyPoints: Point light emits equally in all directions from a single 3D position
Properties: position (x,y,z), intensity, color
No attenuation in basic model, or can have distance-based attenuation

questions.question: Explain the three components of the Phong Illumination Model with formulas.
keyPoints: Ambient: ka*Ia - scattered light illuminating all surfaces equally
Diffuse: kd*Ii*(N·L) - Lambertian reflection from rough surfaces
Specular: ks*Ii*(R·V)^n - mirror-like reflection from shiny surfaces

questions.question: Differentiate between Gouraud Shading and Phong Shading.
keyPoints: Gouraud: interpolates colors/normals at vertices across polygon surface
Phong: interpolates surface normals per pixel, computes lighting at each fragment
Phong produces smoother, more accurate results but is computationally more expensive
Gouraud may miss specular highlights within polygon, Phong captures them

questions.question: What is light attenuation in computer graphics? Explain the standard attenuation formula.
keyPoints: Attenuation models intensity decrease with distance from point light source
Formula: f(d) = 1/(c1 + c2*d + c3*d²)
c1 (constant), c2 (linear), c3 (quadratic) are attenuation coefficients
Higher d values result in lower intensity reaching the surface

questions.question: Define Spotlight and explain its key parameters.
keyPoints: Spotlight emits light within a constrained cone in a specific direction
Key parameters: position, direction (axis of cone), cone angle (spread), falloff exponent
Light intensity decreases from center to edge of cone based on falloff
Used to simulate flashlights, spotlights, automotive headlights

questions.question: Explain Lambert's Cosine Law used in diffuse reflection.
keyPoints: States that diffuse light intensity is proportional to cosine of angle between surface normal and light direction
Mathematically: Id ∝ N·L = |N||L|cos(θ)
When surface faces light directly (θ=0), maximum diffuse light is received
When surface is perpendicular to light, N·L = 0, no diffuse light

questions.question: What is the Blinn-Phong modification to the Phong model? Why is it preferred?
keyPoints: Replaces R·V calculation with H·N where H is halfway vector between L and V
H = normalize(L + V) - direction halfway between light and view
Computationally cheaper than computing reflection vector R
Avoids artifacts when view direction and reflection direction are nearly opposite
Widely used in real-time graphics hardware

questions.question: Explain in detail the different types of light sources used in computer graphics with their mathematical representations and typical use cases.
keyPoints: Point Light: I = position-based, radiates in all directions, use for lightbulbs
Directional Light: parallel rays, defined by direction only, use for sunlight
Spot Light: cone-constrained, parameters include angle and falloff, use for flashlights
Ambient Light: uniform illumination, no direction, prevents pure black areas
Each has position, direction, intensity, color properties
Mathematical representation differs: point uses distance attenuation, directional doesn't, spot uses cone calculations

questions.question: Derive and explain the complete Phong Reflection Model equation. List all variables and explain their significance.
keyPoints: I = ka*Ia + kd*Ii*(N·L) + ks*Ii*(R·V)^n
ka, kd, ks = ambient, diffuse, specular reflectance coefficients (0-1)
Ia, Ii = ambient and incident light intensities
N = surface normal vector (unit)
L = light direction vector (unit, toward light)
R = reflection vector = 2(N·L)N - L
V = view direction vector (unit)
n = shininess exponent (higher = smaller highlights)
Explain each term's contribution to final color

questions.question: Compare Flat Shading, Gouraud Shading, and Phong Shading methods with respect to implementation, computational cost, visual quality, and suitable applications.
keyPoints: Flat: compute once per polygon, fastest, faceted look, suitable for low-poly/retro graphics
Gouraud: compute at vertices, interpolate colors, moderate cost, smooth but may miss specular highlights, standard for real-time
Phong: interpolate normals, compute per pixel, highest quality, captures specular highlights, used in high-quality rendering
Trade-off: quality vs computation time increases in order Flat < Gouraud < Phong
Hardware requirements: Flat/Gouraud can use vertex shader, Phong requires fragment shader capability

questions.question: Explain the concept of specular reflection in detail. How does the shininess exponent affect the appearance of specular highlights? Include the reflection vector calculation.
keyPoints: Specular simulates mirror-like reflection from shiny surfaces
Reflection vector R = 2(N·L)N - L (where L is toward light)
Specular intensity = (R·V)^n where V is view direction
n (shininess exponent) controls highlight size: low n = large diffuse highlight, high n = small sharp highlight
Typical range: n=0 (matte) to n=100+ (highly polished)
View-dependent: highlight appears only when V aligns with R
Creates realistic shiny effects on metals, plastics, wet surfaces
